title: Enhancing Multi-Domain System Simulation with FMI Co-Simulation - Semiwiki
gist: Siemens EDA has added FMI 2.0 co-simulation support to HyperLynx AMS and cloud-based PartQuest Explore, enabling black-box, tool-independent coupling of electrical, mechanical, control, and thermal simulators (Amesim, Twin Activate, Flotherm) for multi-domain system verification.

insights.claim: Siemens has implemented FMI 2.0 co-simulation in HyperLynx AMS and PartQuest Explore, letting each tool export a containerized model (FMU) for coupling across different domains without requiring cross-tool expertise.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: FMI support broadens Siemens' addressable multi-domain simulation market and lowers integration friction for its EDA/simulation portfolio.
insights.quote: The recent implementation of FMI compatibility in PartQuest Explore and HyperLynx AMS opens a large range of co-simulation capabilities
insights.locator: HyperLynx AMS (HL AMS) and PartQuest Explore

insights.claim: As of 2026 there are over 270 FMI-compatible tools spanning many fields, and FMI 2.0 is the most widely adopted version as of 2025, with Siemens a member of the FMI Steering Committee.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: A 270+ tool ecosystem makes FMI the de facto interoperability standard, a moat-neutralizer that favors tools that support it.
insights.quote: there are over 270 FMI-compatible tools spanning many different fields, disciplines, companies, and organizations

insights.claim: The FMI standard's tool-independence lets a specialist in one tool run co-simulation without expertise in another, and black-box FMUs keep internal logic, equations, and algorithms opaque to protect IP and sensitive data.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: Black-box IP protection removes a key barrier to cross-company co-simulation, enabling supplier-OEM model sharing.
insights.quote: black-box models can be used to keep internal logic, equations, and algorithms opaque to the user

insights.claim: During co-simulation the FMU and importing model exchange data at a communication time-step that must be tuned to balance accuracy against runtime, and it need not equal the tools' internal time-steps.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: The communication time-step is the primary accuracy/runtime lever and first place to debug wrong results.
insights.quote: If the results of a co‑simulation seem infeasible or incorrect, the value of the co-simulation time step is a good place to look

insights.claim: Appropriate time-steps scaled with simulated duration: 1 ms for a 5-second electromechanical run, 1 microsecond for a 30 ms power-converter control loop, and 100 ms for an hour-long thermal simulation.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: Concrete time-step guidance across use cases reduces setup trial-and-error for engineers adopting the flow.

insights.claim: HyperLynx AMS co-simulated with Simcenter Amesim to drive a stepper-motor-and-winch electromechanical system, and with Altair Twin Activate (and MathWorks Simulink) for control-system power-converter loops.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: Demonstrated interoperability with Amesim, Twin Activate, and Simulink extends Siemens' reach into mechatronics and control design workflows.

insights.claim: PartQuest Explore co-simulated with Simcenter Flotherm CFD to thermally analyze a smartphone board across idle/streaming/gaming usage states, with the outer case stabilizing near 37 degrees Celsius during gaming.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: Coupling electrical usage models to CFD thermal solvers enables realistic system-level thermal verification, a differentiator for board design tools.

insights.claim: Flotherm can export BCI-ROM models in VHDL-AMS format for PartQuest Explore and also export FMUs, giving FMI-compatible tools direct access to its CFD solver.
insights.speaker: Admin
insights.so_what: Multiple export paths (BCI-ROM and FMU) reduce lock-in and ease adoption of Siemens thermal models in mixed toolchains.
